w:r.w:t: End-to-End Implementation Using TigerGraph Savanna MCP
“30–40M phantom voters. The graph sees them.”
Powered by TigerGraph Savanna MCP | GSQL | REST++ | React + D3.js | FastAPI

India's electoral rolls are public. They contain each voter's name, age, address, booth assignment, and family relationship codes. Individually, every entry looks routine. In a graph, the anomalies scream: 50 voters sharing a single room, families where the son is older than the father, entire booths that appeared overnight, or one address that spans three different pin codes.

The Ghost Voter Network Detector uses TigerGraph Savanna via Model Context Protocol (MCP) to ingest electoral roll data, model voter relationships as a connected graph, and run six coordinated detection algorithms in under one second per constituency query. The result: a ranked, evidence-backed list of ghost voter clusters, complete with PDF evidence packages ready for submission to the Election Commission of India (ECI).

SQL can find 50 people at one address. Only a graph can find the coordinated multi-address, multi-booth network behind it — tracing the spider web of shared devices, impossible family trees, and overnight batch enrollments that prove systematic, organised fraud rather than data entry errors.

Multiple independent studies and ECI internal audits estimate 30-40 million duplicate, phantom, or fraudulently enrolled voters in India's electoral rolls across states. These are not typos — they are coordinated enrollments designed to enable booth-level manipulation.
Son older than father in roll
ECI's internal SQL tools find individual anomalies but cannot trace the network connecting them
Manual audits process thousands of entries per day — the fraud operates at millions
Traditional deduplication matches exact strings — fraudsters use phonetic variants across scripts
No tool currently correlates address data with Aadhaar seeding status, device fingerprints, and temporal batch signatures simultaneously

TigerGraph Savanna exposes a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that gives Claude and the application direct access to graph operations — schema management, data loading, GSQL execution, and built-in algorithm invocation — all through a structured tool interface.
Run schema creation, detection queries, scoring jobs
Load voters, addresses, booths from ETL pipeline
Execute installed GSQL queries with parameters
PageRank, Louvain, WCC, Betweenness on graph
Inspect current graph schema during development
Trigger bulk CSV loads from S3 or local storage
CREATE VERTEX Constituency (PRIMARY_ID const_id STRING, name STRING, state STRING);
CREATE VERTEX PinCode (PRIMARY_ID pin STRING, district STRING, state STRING);
CREATE VERTEX PhoneNumber (PRIMARY_ID phone STRING, operator STRING);
CREATE VERTEX Device (PRIMARY_ID device_fp STRING, type STRING);
CREATE DIRECTED EDGE ASSIGNED_TO (FROM Voter, TO Booth, since_date DATETIME);
CREATE UNDIRECTED EDGE SHARES_ADDRESS (FROM Voter, TO Voter, co_residents INT);
CREATE UNDIRECTED EDGE SHARES_DEVICE (FROM Voter, TO Voter, device_fp STRING);
CREATE UNDIRECTED EDGE SHARES_PHONE (FROM Voter, TO Voter, phone STRING);
CREATE DIRECTED EDGE IN_CONSTITUENCY (FROM Booth, TO Constituency);
CREATE DIRECTED EDGE PIN_BELONGS_TO (FROM Address, TO PinCode);
CREATE DIRECTED EDGE USES_PHONE (FROM Voter, TO PhoneNumber);
CREATE DIRECTED EDGE USES_DEVICE (FROM Voter, TO Device);
# Stage 3: OCR for scanned PDFs (Devanagari / Tamil / Telugu)
# Phonetic near-duplicates: same soundex + DOB within 365 days
# Or direct REST++ for high-volume batches
'''Idempotent upsert — re-running does not duplicate data'''
for i in range(0, len(voter_records), batch_size):

All six queries are installed on TigerGraph Savanna and invoked via MCP tool query_run or REST++ endpoint. Each returns a ranked list of anomalies with evidence fields for the frontend and PDF export.
CREATE QUERY address_overcrowding(INT threshold = 10) FOR GRAPH GhostVoterGraph {
Voters = SELECT a FROM Addresses:a -(REGISTERED_AT:e)- Voter:v
// Return overcrowded addresses ranked by density
CREATE QUERY impossible_families() FOR GRAPH GhostVoterGraph {
Families = SELECT v FROM Voters:v -(FAMILY_OF:e)- Voter:u
(e.relation_type == "brother" AND v.age == u.age AND v.name != u.name)
CREATE QUERY multi_booth_ghost(VERTEX<Address> start_addr, INT max_hops = 3)
Step1 = SELECT v FROM Seed:a -(REGISTERED_AT)- Voter:v
Step2 = SELECT b FROM Step1:v -(ASSIGNED_TO)- Booth:b
Ghosts = SELECT v FROM Step1:v WHERE v.@is_ghost == TRUE;
CREATE QUERY overnight_batch(DATETIME window_start, DATETIME window_end,
// Count additions within time window per booth
WindowVoters = SELECT b FROM Booths:b -(ASSIGNED_TO:e)- Voter:v
WHERE v.added_date BETWEEN window_start AND window_end
// Flag booths where addition rate is anomalous
CREATE QUERY cross_pincode_fraud() FOR GRAPH GhostVoterGraph {
// Voters at same address claiming different pin codes
CoResidents = SELECT a FROM Addresses:a -(REGISTERED_AT)- Voter:v
Fraud = SELECT a FROM Addresses:a WHERE a.@pin_fraud == TRUE
// Master query — runs all detections, returns sorted ghost clusters
Const = SELECT c FROM Constituency:c WHERE c.name == constituency_name;
Booths = SELECT b FROM Const:c -(IN_CONSTITUENCY)- Booth:b;
Voters = SELECT v FROM Booths:b -(ASSIGNED_TO)- Voter:v;

Each voter vertex carries a risk_score attribute (0-100) that is the sum of weighted signals. The score is computed by a scheduled GSQL job running nightly via TigerGraph Savanna's built-in scheduler.
CREATE QUERY nightly_score_update() FOR GRAPH GhostVoterGraph {
UPDATE v FROM Voters:v SET v.risk_score = 0;
S1 = SELECT v FROM Voters:v -(REGISTERED_AT)- Address:a
S2 = SELECT v FROM Voters:v -(DUPLICATE_OF)- Voter:u
S3 = SELECT v FROM Voters:v -(FAMILY_OF:e)- Voter:u
S4 = SELECT v FROM Voters:v WHERE v.aadhaar_linked == FALSE
UPDATE v FROM Voters:v SET v.risk_score = min(v.risk_score, 100.0);
// Schedule via Savanna: run at 2 AM IST daily
All algorithms below are invoked via MCP tool algorithm_run or the Savanna Graph Data Science library endpoint. No custom implementation needed — Savanna ships these out of the box.

Addresses connected to many suspicious voters score high. Reveals hub addresses — single rooms that spawn ghost networks across booths. High-PageRank addresses are the origin points of coordinated fraud.

Clusters voters by their address, family, device, and phone edges. Tight clusters with high internal density indicate ghost factories — coordinated groups enrolled as if they are a community but with no real-world basis. Run per constituency to keep computation bounded.
// Post-process: communities where avg(risk_score) > 60 = ghost cluster
Finds isolated ghost networks — groups of voters with no legitimate connections outside the component. These are the cleanest fraud clusters: internal connections only, no ties to verified voters.

Finds key connector voters who bridge multiple ghost clusters. These individuals are often the actual human agents managing multiple fake roll submissions. High betweenness centrality + high risk_score = top investigation priority.
Two booths with >80% voter overlap but different constituencies indicate ward boundary manipulation — the same pool of voters assigned to different booths to inflate numbers in both.
REST++ wrapper, JWT auth, Redis cache

1. Type constituency name in search box 2. TigerGraph query fires via MCP in < 1 second 3. D3.js force graph renders — ghost clusters glow red, suspicious nodes amber, clean voters gray 4. Click a red cluster → side panel shows the full evidence: address, voter list, anomaly breakdown, family tree, batch timestamps 5. Click Export → PDF evidence package downloads 6. The graph sees what SQL cannot.
# Run via MCP or direct GSQL
export default function GhostGraph({ nodes, links }) {

Export graph embeddings from TigerGraph via GraphSAGE. Train an isolation forest on voter subgraph vectors. This catches subtle coordinated patterns that rule-based queries miss — e.g., groups that individually score 40-50 but whose subgraph topology is statistically identical to confirmed ghost clusters.
for voter_id, score in zip(voter_ids, scores):

Poll the ECI voter roll update API on a 6-hour cycle. When new voters are added, immediately run the anomaly check. If the new batch is at an existing ghost-flagged address or scores > 60 at registration time, trigger an alert to the dashboard before the roll is published.
Webhook receiver on FastAPI listens for ECI update events
New voter batch runs through all 6 detection queries in sequence
Batches with Louvain community matching existing ghost cluster trigger immediate red alert
Temporal signature check: if 100+ voters added with same batch_id within 60 seconds → programmatic fraud alert

A GSQL query that runs across constituency boundaries — something no single-constituency SQL tool can do. It finds the same physical address appearing simultaneously in two different constituency rolls, feeding ghost voters into multiple constituencies from a single source location.

Overlay ECI-published turnout data with ghost scores. The two fraud signatures are complementary: booths with 90%+ turnout AND high ghost scores indicate booth capturing (real votes cast for ghost identities). Booths with 0% turnout for specific voter clusters indicate pure ghost registrations — the voters were never intended to actually vote.

A public endpoint: submit suspected ghost voter EPIC ID and receive an anonymized evidence summary showing which anomaly signals were detected. No PII exposed. Rate-limited to 10 requests/minute per IP. Designed for citizen journalists, opposition parties, and election observers.

Many state electoral rolls are published only as scanned PDFs in regional scripts. The pipeline handles: Devanagari (Hindi, Marathi), Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Malayalam, Odia. Tesseract with language packs + IndicBERT for post-OCR name normalization across scripts — critical for detecting the same name enrolled in different script forms.

A React slider across election cycles (2014, 2019, 2024 general elections). Watch ghost clusters appear and disappear by year. This temporal view provides the strongest legal evidence: a ghost cluster that materializes 3 months before a state election and disappears after it is definitional electoral fraud.
Sign up at console.tgcloud.io, create a new workspace
Choose region: Mumbai (ap-south-1) for lowest latency to ECI data
Enable Graph Data Science library (required for PageRank, Louvain, WCC)
Generate API token: Settings > Credentials > Create Token
Set MCP config with Savanna host URL and bearer token
Run schema creation GSQL (Section 3.2) via GSQL Studio or MCP gsql_execute
Configure scheduled query for nightly_score_update at 2 AM IST
Yes + traces the network behind it

If deployed at national scale across all 543 Lok Sabha constituencies: 30-40M phantom voters identified with multi-signal evidence. Each flagged cluster generates a submission-ready PDF for ECI review. The temporal analysis can prove whether ghost enrollments correlate with election schedules — the strongest possible legal evidence of organised electoral fraud.
Open the dashboard. Map shows India. Districts colored by ghost density — dark red = high fraud concentration.
Click a dark red district. Zoom to constituency list. Select top-ranked constituency.
D3 graph renders in under 1 second. Ghost clusters glow red. Clean voters are gray. The network is visible.
Click the largest red cluster. Side panel: 73 voters. One address. Three different pin codes. 47 added in 900ms batch. Zero Aadhaar links. Score: 97/100.
Click Export. PDF downloads in 3 seconds: voter list, anomaly evidence, graph screenshot, legal summary. Ready for ECI.
Switch to Timeline. Drag slider from 2019 to 2024. Watch the cluster appear 90 days before election date. This is the smoking gun.
The graph sees what SQL cannot. And now, so can the Election Commission.
